channel.title: torontotrees
channel.description: Essays and visualizations built on Toronto's open street-tree data.

item.title: The caterpillar test — why a Toronto oak is worth a hundred ginkgos
item.description: Doug Tallamy

item.title: Three trees I can't explain — anomalies in Toronto's street-tree dataset
item.description: A 250-centimetre hawthorn that physically can

item.title: What Kyoto's monks know that Toronto's dataset doesn't
item.description: Yasuyuki Aono

item.title: The Norway-maple paradox — Toronto's most-planted tree is the one the city no longer plants
item.description: 99,931 Norway maples line Toronto

item.title: The narrow-street advantage — why Toronto's old grid carries 4× the trees of its new condos
item.description: The densest 200m stretch of street trees in Toronto has forty. The sparsest has three. The difference isn

item.title: The ravines — the other half of Toronto's canopy
item.description: 689,013 city-owned street trees are on a map. An estimated 5 to 8 million more live in Toronto

item.title: A tale of two Victorians — Cabbagetown vs. Trinity-Bellwoods
item.description: Two old downtown Toronto neighbourhoods, same era, same rowhouse DNA, same gentrification arc. One has 45% canopy. The other has 20%. Here

item.title: Toronto's veteran street trees — a hall of fame
item.description: The single largest specimen of each of Toronto

item.title: Toronto's street trees are worth $20 million a year
item.description: Applying published ecosystem-service equations to each of 686,000 Toronto street trees — totalling $20M/year in carbon, stormwater, air quality, and cooling benefits. And an 80× gap between neighbourhoods.

item.title: The west-end cherry belt — where to see cherry blossoms in Toronto
item.description: High Park is famous for cherry blossoms. But the data says Oakwood Village has more — and nine times as many cherries line the west-end blocks around St Clair.

item.title: Toronto's heat islands aren't where you'd expect
item.description: An impervious-minus-canopy heat proxy maps the city

item.title: The honey locust — Toronto's civic workhorse
item.description: Gleditsia triacanthos is Toronto

item.title: Beyond the curb — Toronto's actual canopy
item.description: Toronto

item.title: Every street tree in Toronto, as a dot
item.description: A pointillist portrait of Toronto: 689,013 city-owned street trees, coloured by genus. Plus a map showing just the 69,563 Norway maples — and why that matters.

item.title: Toronto's rarest street trees — a pilgrimage guide
item.description: Seven tree species have exactly one specimen in all of Toronto

item.title: The ginkgo — Toronto's 17,474 living fossils
item.description: Ginkgo biloba is the sole survivor of an entire plant division that predates dinosaurs. Toronto is quietly planting tens of thousands of them, almost all under 15 years old.

item.title: The black locust — Toronto's 2,943 Robinia pseudoacacia
item.description: Black locust is one of Toronto

item.title: A decade of standing still — how Toronto's canopy survived the ash catastrophe
item.description: Between 2008 and 2018, Toronto lost an estimated 80,000 mature ash trees to emerald ash borer. Over the same decade, citywide canopy cover went from 25.84% to 25.89%. Here

item.title: Toronto's canopy is unequally shared — torontotrees
item.description: Four findings from an afternoon with Toronto

item.title: What the trees know
item.description: Between the data and the product is the reason. A reflection on Toronto
